g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about the Scope Resolution Operator in C++, a fundamental tool for managing variable and function scopes.
g.text: The Scope Resolution Operator (::) in C++
Understanding :: for Global, Class, and Namespace Access

g.data-narration: The scope resolution operator (::) is a unary operator that provides explicit access to entities in different scopes like global variables, class members, and namespace members.
g.text: What is the Scope Resolution Operator?
The :: operator provides explicit access to entities in different scopes:

g.data-narration: The operator is essential because it resolves naming conflicts, enables separation of interface and implementation, and controls name visibility in complex programs.
g.text: The scope resolution operator solves critical programming challenges:

g.data-narration: When a local variable has the same name as a global variable, the local one hides the global. Use :: to explicitly access the global variable.
g.text: When local and global variables share the same name, :: accesses the global:
Prefix with :: to access global scope

g.data-narration: In this code example, local marks=95 takes precedence, but ::marks accesses the global marks=85, showing the difference clearly.
Use ClassName:: to define member functions outside the class. This separates interface in header files from implementation in source files.
g.text: When defining functions outside the class, use ClassName:: to associate with the class:
public: void setDimensions(double l, double w);

g.data-narration: The BankAccount class demonstrates practical use of :: to define functions outside, creating clean separation between class declaration and implementation.
g.text: The :: links function to class scope

g.data-narration: Static data members require both declaration inside the class AND definition outside using ClassName:: for initialization.
g.text: Static members need BOTH declaration (in class) AND definition (outside class):

g.data-narration: Static member functions are accessed using ClassName::functionName from outside the class, not through objects.
g.text: Access static members using ClassName:: (not through objects):

g.data-narration: The Student class tracks total students using a static counter. Each object increments it, demonstrating practical static member usage.
g.text: Example: Student Class with Static Counter

g.data-narration: Namespaces organize code to prevent name conflicts. Use NamespaceName:: to access members, as shown with MathOperations::add.
g.text: Namespaces organize code and prevent naming conflicts:
int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
Use namespace::member to access namespace contents

g.data-narration: The std:: prefix is namespace resolution for the C++ Standard Library. It's required when not using 'using namespace std'.
g.text: All standard library elements are in the std namespace:
Or use: using namespace std; to avoid prefix

g.data-narration: Enumerations nested in classes require ClassName::EnumValue to access their values, as shown with Color::Red.
g.text: When enums are nested in classes, access using ClassName::EnumValue:
public: enum Primary { Red=1, Green=2, Blue=3 };
Without class: just Primary::Red or using enum

g.data-narration: This table summarizes all uses of the scope resolution operator: global access, class members, namespaces, and static definitions.
Key takeaways: Always use :: for global access, member function definitions, static members, namespaces, and nested enums.
Avoid common mistakes: double colon (::) not single, always define static members outside, and use ClassName:: for member functions.

g.text: Omitting class name in function def
